title: A Toccata Of Galuppiן¿½s
content:
I

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh Galuppi, Baldassaro, this is very sad to find!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I can hardly misconceive you; it would prove me deaf and blind;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But although I take your meaning, ן¿½tis with such a heavy mind!

II

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here you come with your old music, and hereן¿½s all the good it brings.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What, they lived once thus at Venice where the merchants were the kings,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where Saint Markן¿½s is, where the Doges used to wed the sea with rings?

III

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ay, because the seaן¿½s the street there; and ן¿½tis arched by . . . what you call
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ . . . Shylockן¿½s bridge with houses on it, where they kept the carnival:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I was never out of England, itן¿½s as if I saw it all.

IV

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did young people take their pleasure when the sea was warm in May?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Balls and masks begun at midnight, burning ever to mid-day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When they made up fresh adventures for the morrow, do you say?

V

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was a lady such a lady, cheeks so round and lips so red,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On her neck the small face buoyant, like a bell-flower on its bed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oן¿½er the breastן¿½s superb abundance where a man might base his head?

VI

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, and it was graceful of them, theyן¿½d break talk off and afford
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She, to bite her maskן¿½s black velvet, he, to finger on his sword,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While you sat and played Toccatas, stately at the clavichord?

VII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What? Those lesser thirds so plaintive, sixths diminished, sigh on sigh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Told them something? Those suspensions, those solutions, ן¿½Must we die?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Those commiserating sevenths, ן¿½Life might last! we can but try!

VIII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Were you happy?ן¿½ ן¿½Yes.ן¿½ ן¿½And are you still as happy?ן¿½ ן¿½Yes. And you?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Then, more kisses!ן¿½ ן¿½Did I stop them, when a million seemed so few?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hark, the dominantן¿½s persistence till it must be answered to!

IX

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So, an octave struck the answer. Oh, they praised you, I dare say!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Brave Galuppi! that was music! good alike at grave and gay!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I can always leave off talking when I hear a master play!ן¿½

X

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then they left you for their pleasure: till in due time, one by one,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some with lives that came to nothing, some with deeds as well undone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Death stepped tacitly and took them where they never see the sun.

XI

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But when I sit down to reason, think to take my stand nor swerve,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While I triumph oן¿½er a secret wrung from natureן¿½s close reserve,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In you come with your cold music till I creep throן¿½ every nerve.

XII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yes, you, like a ghostly cricket, creaking where a house was burned:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Dust and ashes, dead and done with, Venice spent what Venice earned.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The soul, doubtless, is immortal, where a soul can be discerned.

XIII

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yours for instance: you know physics, something of geology,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Mathematics are your pastime; souls shall rise in their degree;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Butterflies may dread extinction, youן¿½ll not die, it cannot be!

XIV

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As for Venice and her people, merely born to bloom and drop,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Here on earth they bore their fruitage, mirth and folly were the crop:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What of soul was left, I wonder, when the kissing had to stop?

XV

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Dust and ashes!ן¿½ So you creak it, and I want the heart to scold.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dear dead women, with such hair, too, whatן¿½s become of all the gold
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Used to hang and brush their bosoms? I feel chilly and grown old.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Browning represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Robert Browning's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
